title: Tax evasion (The Behavioral Background)
abstract: Our research aimed to explore the “black box” of taxpayers’ decision-making in an attempt to identify generalizable patterns of behaviour. The analysis was based on the published decisions of the Curia from 2018 to 2023. The information from the judgments was transferred into the statistical data processing system, creating 39 variables. The variables and their correlations were analyzed using the statistical program SPSS. The statistical sample included 120 review judgments, which were representative of the review cases of the period. In addition to the statistical empirical evidence, our study processed models from the literature on tax avoidance and reconstructed individual tax rate positions.
summary_en: Why do some Hungarian taxpayers evade, and what does it take for the Curia (Hungarian Supreme Court) to overturn a tax-fraud judgement? This paper analyses 120 Curia review decisions on tax matters between 2018 and 2023, extracts 39 variables per case, and uses statistical techniques to identify patterns. The analysis combines empirical patterns with theoretical models of tax avoidance to reconstruct the logic of individual tax positions. The paper is an early attempt to bring data analysis into Hungarian judicial review of tax disputes.
data_used: Hungarian Supreme Court (Curia) published review decisions on tax matters, 2018–2023; 120 cases representative of the population; 39 variables per case hand-coded from judgement text.
policy_relevance: For the Hungarian Tax Administration (NAV) and Ministry of Finance: characterising the patterns in overturned tax-evasion cases is useful for both enforcement strategy (which cases are winnable?) and for legal-policy reform (which rules produce ambiguous outcomes?). Also relevant for legal academics evaluating judicial consistency. Small sample (120 cases); a larger panel or NLP-based analysis of all Curia decisions would strengthen the findings.
